posts.cooked:
I hosted a dinner party last night with several close friends. Each of them is really into wine, but not so much that they would peruse the Berserker forum.

Each of my friends brought a bottle of their choice. I chose the 2021 Maximin Grunhaus Schloss Riesling from the Von Schubert estate. I sourced it locally for about $30.

Needless to say, the wine was very underwhelming. Upon opening the bottle (screwcap), there was a strong aroma consistent with riesling. However, it was fairly pungent and slightly off-putting. It took about 15 minutes to blow off. I’ve never experienced an off-putting aroma from a young riesling before. It wasn’t corked because it had a screwcap. I suspected the wine was maybe flawed, although it eventually blew off and drank well.

Overall, the wine was uninteresting and had a short finish. It was labeled as dry, but clearly had a detectable amount of RS. It got better over the course of the evening, but never really took off. I was hoping for more from this estate. There was no complexity. It had good acid and was well-balanced. It was rather quaffable, but not something to ponder. I certainly didn’t feel it to be worth the $30 price, and possibly not reflective of the Von Schubert estate.

I was so unimpressed I decided to pull out an $8 bottle of 2022 Chateau Ste. Michelle’s (CSM) Dry Riesling and do a blind tasting. 3 out of 4 guests who did the blind tasting preferred the $8 Ste. Michelle. CSM has obviously begun to spritz their riesling with some co2, giving it a slight sparkling effect on the palette. The aroma was simple, but did not have any pungent characteristics. It was right down the middle, and was honestly pretty good. I did not personally feel that CSM was better, but it definitely was not worse. For $8, it held its own and seems to be a great value.

All in all, I was hugely disappointed. Perhaps my friends and I are just a bunch of rubes who can’t properly discern wine. Or maybe the Schloss bottling from Maximin Grunhaus is really not worth the $30 price tag. CSM was just as good for $8.

Curious to know what others think and what their experiences are!

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
Maybe reduction? I always splash decant young Riesling and try to give them as many hours of air as possible. Trocken typically does still contain some RS.

However, something to note is this is a very entry level bottle from Von Schubert. I’m not sure I would expect to be wowed or for the wine to take off. Looking at CT, most people paid under $13 for this bottle, so I might agree you should be expecting a bit more for $30.

Thanks for the reply.

I would not be surprised if I overpaid for this bottle. My local shop is known for their high prices. I could see where a $13 bottle would be on par with the $8 one. Some of the prices on their bottles are in line with restaurant prices. Perhaps that is where I went wrong.

posts.name: Thomas Keim
posts.cooked:
Maximin Grunhaus has been my favorite Riesling producer in Germany for well over 40 years, and I have yet to have a mediocre Riesling from them (at any level). Keep in mind, the Grunhaus is located on the Ruwer, near the Saar, and these wines take time to develop - even the entry level ones. They can be very chiseled in their youth (which is one of the attributes I adore with Grunhaus Rieslings) and will never be “showy”, fat wines.

But for someone used to New World, American Rieslings, I can see the Schloss Riesling being understated next to a mass produced American Riesling (that probably saw a little force carbonation).

posts.name: Ilkka |_ |_| mme
posts.cooked:
Andrew_K:

However, something to note is this is a very entry level bottle from Von Schubert. I’m not sure I would expect to be wowed or for the wine to take off. Looking at CT, most people paid under $13 for this bottle, so I might agree you should be expecting a bit more for $30.

Very much all of this. The dry or dryish entry level Mosel wines rarely are exciting even from good producers. I realize that it doesn’t make sense to compare US and EU prices but the fantastic Kabinetten and Spätlesen cost only ~20€ in Germany so $30 for the entry level wine seems like a lot.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Andrew_K:

Maybe reduction?

Most likely this. Many Rieslings from great German producers are prone to Böckser aka. sponti funk aka. skunky reduction. That’s just free sulfur-containing molecules that dissipate with oxygen. It can start off smoky, gaseous or rubbery, but the more there is, the more it just to smells like sulfur (first hard-boiled eggs, then farts). If it gets to sewage or rotting vegetables, then it’s mostly more stable mercaptans that don’t blow off that easily and by that point the wine is basically flawed - although I can’t remember ever getting a German Riesling that had gone that bad. Some other wines, yes.

Getting rid of reduction is one of the main reasons I like to age my Rieslings a lot - it tends to disappear with enough cellar age. You can also get rid of reduction by just decanting the wine and letting it aerate - although very heavily reduced wines can take a ridiculous amount of time before they blow off their skunky notes. I’ve had a von Othegraven kabinett that was basically undrinkable due to its heavily sulfurous nose and even a good deal of aeration did not help. I just recorked the wine and put it back into the fridge, sampling it every few days. It took more than a week before the sulfurous character had mostly subsided.

posts.name: Lars Carlberg
posts.cooked:
I agree; it’s probably a reduced note. But I haven’t tasted that entry-level wine from Maximin Grünhaus.

Back in 2014, I published an article titled “What’s the Stink?”

link_counts.title: What’s the Stink? – Lars Carlberg: Mosel Wine

posts.name: Lars Carlberg
posts.cooked: Joh. Jos. Prüm, von Othegraven, Schloss Lieser, Nik Weis, and Schäfer-Fröhlich often have this sponti stink in past vintages.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
IlkkaL:

I realize that it doesn’t make sense to compare US and EU prices but the fantastic Kabinetten and Spätlesen cost only ~20€ in Germany so $30 for the entry level wine seems like a lot.

That was my thinking as well. The last Grunhaus I bought was from the 2019 vintage (and I bought a good bit of assorted wines from the vintage) and I paid about $30 for the Herrenberg Kabinett. The 2019 Herrenberg Kabinett is just a drop dead gorgeous wine.

I have never seen or tasted the Schloss Riesling from Grunhaus so I cannot comment on the quality of the wine. But, if you want something really good from the estate to try, try a Kabinett or Spatlese from either Herrenberg or Abtsberg.

I cannot say that Grunhaus is my favorite producer of German wines, but it clearly is in the top four - with JJ Prum, Rheinhold Haart and Zilliken (hard to put these four in any order). I have also loved the wines for about 40 years - I think my first wines from them were from the 1983 vintage.

Also, a question. While 2019 was a warmer vintage in the MSR, 2021 is a cooler vintage. I have had enough 2021s to know that from the right vineyard source the wines can be fabulous. But, are the wines from lesser vineyards less good in 2021 than in say a vintage like 2019? Could the grapes get sufficiently ripe?

posts.name: D@vid K@ntr0witz
posts.cooked: An entry-level QbA Ruwer trocken from 2021 sounds like an extremely challenging drink.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
RylanJacobs:

CSM has obviously begun to spritz their riesling with some co2, giving it a slight sparkling effect on the palette.

I know this is an ancillary point within the thread, but when young riesling (and sometimes other light bodied white wines) has a light spritz character, I always assumed that was something left over from the fermentation process.

Am I correct, or do any legit producers actually carbonate the wine by adding CO2?

posts.name: Lars Carlberg
posts.cooked: I’m glad you asked. Many estates and contract bottlers, and not only in the Mosel, use a device, such as Carbofresh, to inject CO2, but the taste isn’t quite the same as with residual CO2 from fermentation.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
Lars_Carlberg:

I’m glad you asked. Many estates and contract bottlers, and not only in the Mosel, use a device, such as Carbofresh, to inject CO2, but the taste isn’t quite the same as with residual CO2 from fermentation.

Wow. A few follow-up questions for you or anyone else who knows:

(1) is the goal to make the wine taste spritzy, or is it as a preservative or something else?

(2) is the wine intended to remain spritzy as it ages, or only to be that way in its early years?

(3) do they generally do this for their inexpensive bottlings, or do they do this for their mid and upper tier offerings too?

Thanks.

posts.name: Lars Carlberg
posts.cooked: The main goal is to replace the small amount of CO2 that is lost at bottling. The added spritz decreases as the wine matures in bottle. For those who add CO2, it’s usually for all their bottlings.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: You should tell them about pressurized vats to arrest the fermentation, too! I never actually saw one of those in the flesh…

posts.name: Lars Carlberg
posts.cooked: That’s a great point. Maximin Grünhaus and Carl Schmitt-Wagner once used pressurized tanks.

posts.name: Yule Kim
posts.cooked:
I remember Prum had a reputation for intense sponti/sulfur aromas when young, but I haven’t noticed it as much with more recent vintages.

Similarly with Schloss Lieser – maybe a touch of sponti, but nothing overbearing.

I haven’t opened any Schafer Frohlich recently, but reading notes, it doesn’t seem like the sponti/sulfur aromas I remember reading about 10 years ago is affecting them either.

I’m wondering what is causing the changes? Winemaking changes? Less sulfur? Climate change?

posts.name: Lars Carlberg
posts.cooked:
Yes, that’s true. The wines from J.J. Prüm had more sponti stink in the past. I can only speculate on why. It seems that they made some slight changes in their winemaking. It could be less sulfur. I really don’t know.

As for Schloss Lieser and Schäfer-Fröhlich, I don’t know, either. Perhaps they took measures against the stink.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Prüm as well, no?

posts.name: Lars Carlberg
posts.cooked: As far as I know, J.J. Prüm has standard stainless-steel tanks. They don’t let anyone in the cellar, so I could only double-check this with a friend who worked there for several years. I onced asked Katharina once about it. She said the same. Some have speculated that they have fiberglass tanks.

links.title: What’s the Stink? – Lars Carlberg: Mosel Wine
